messages.content: random thing i've always wondered, why do we get hiccups? like what's the actual point of them

The honest answer is that nobody's quite sure — hiccups seem to be a reflex without a clear modern purpose. The leading theory is that it's an evolutionary leftover, something that may have helped our ancient ancestors clear water from their gills before lungs fully took over.

What's strange is that the reflex arc is elaborate for something so useless — it involves the diaphragm, the vagus nerve, a sudden glottal closure. All that wiring for something that just annoys you for ten minutes.
my niece had them forever yesterday and it got me curious about the whole thing

Kids seem to get the really stubborn ones — there's something almost unfair about it.

It makes sense that watching someone else hiccup would send you down that rabbit hole. Did the curiosity stop at hiccups, or did you end up somewhere weirder on the internet?
i swear everyone's got their own folk cure too. my grandma always did the spoonful of sugar thing

The sugar one is actually one of the more plausible folk cures — the theory is that swallowing something with texture might reset the vagus nerve, which is the same nerve driving the whole reflex. So your grandma may have been onto something without knowing the mechanism.

The holding-your-breath ones probably work by raising CO2 levels, which can interrupt the spasm. Most of them are just different ways of distracting or resetting the same nervous system loop.

What did she swear by — did it actually work, or was it more of a ritual thing?
it's kinda funny how something so common is apparently still a bit of a mystery science-wise

It's a good reminder that "common" and "understood" aren't the same thing. Yawning is similar — incredibly familiar, happens to everyone, and scientists still argue about why.

There's something almost reassuring about that. Not everything ordinary has been fully explained away.
